title: Writing Tips: FCE
intro: General information about the FCE Writing paper, grading criteria, and how to gain or avoid losing marks.

intro_sections.title: FCE Writing paper: time and word limits
intro_sections.content:
**Time:** You have 1 hour 20 minutes for the whole Writing paper.

**Part 1 (compulsory):** Essay, 140–190 words.

**Part 2 (choose one):** Letter/email, article, review, or story – 140–190 words each.

Staying within the word range matters; going far under or over can cost marks. Short forms (e.g. don't) count as one word.

intro_sections.title: FCE task types: essay, letter, article, review, story
intro_sections.content:
**Essay (Part 1):** Discuss the topic; give your opinion; often two sides + your view. Formal/neutral. Clear paragraphs (e.g. intro, points for, points against, conclusion).

**Letter/email (Part 2):** Check instructions for register (formal vs informal). Include greeting and sign-off. Cover all bullet points.

**Article (Part 2):** Title; engaging opening; clear paragraphs; often for a magazine/website. Register as specified.

**Review (Part 2):** Book, film, place, etc. Describe and give your opinion. Title optional but useful.

**Story (Part 2):** Narrative; past tenses; clear beginning, middle and end.

intro_sections.title: How your writing is marked: the four criteria
intro_sections.content:
Each criterion is equally weighted (5 marks each at FCE).

**Content** – Did you do the task? All bullet points covered? Relevant ideas?

**Communicative Achievement** – Right register (formal/informal)? Correct format (letter, article, etc.)? Reader informed and engaged?

**Organisation** – Clear structure? Paragraphs? Linking words? Logical order?

**Language** – Range and accuracy of grammar and vocabulary. Spelling and punctuation matter.

intro_sections.title: Examples: what helps and what hurts your mark
intro_sections.content:
**Weak:** One long paragraph with no linkers – Organisation and readability suffer.

**Strong:** Clear paragraphs and linkers (e.g. However, In addition) – Organisation is strong.

**Weak:** Very informal tone in a task that asks for a formal reply – Communicative Achievement is affected.

**Strong:** Appropriate register and format (greeting, sign-off, paragraphs) – Communicative Achievement is met.

intro_sections.title: How to get a good mark
intro_sections.content:
**Content:** Read the task; cover every bullet; add ideas where asked.

**Communicative Achievement:** Match register and format; use correct layout (greeting/sign-off for letters).

**Organisation:** Plan first; one main idea per paragraph; use linkers (however, in addition, as a result).

**Language:** Vary grammar and vocabulary; check spelling and punctuation; avoid repeated errors.

intro_sections.title: What to avoid
intro_sections.content:
**Content:** Don't go off-topic; don't skip bullets; don't write too little or far over the limit.

**Communicative Achievement:** Don't use wrong register or format (e.g. no greeting in a letter).

**Organisation:** Avoid one huge paragraph or only "and" or "also".

**Language:** Avoid repeated mistakes and very basic vocabulary if aiming high; watch fixed phrases (e.g. at the end vs in the end).

quiz.question: What is the word range for the FCE Part 1 essay?
How long do you have for the FCE Writing paper?
Which is one of the four marking criteria for FCE Writing?
quiz.explanation: The four are Content, Communicative Achievement, Organisation, Language.

quiz.question: What does 'Content' mainly check?

options.b: Whether you covered the task and all points

quiz.explanation: Content checks if you did the task and addressed all bullet points.

quiz.question: What does 'Communicative Achievement' include?
quiz.explanation: It includes register, format, and whether the reader is informed and engaged.

quiz.question: Which helps your 'Organisation' mark?
quiz.explanation: Clear structure, paragraphs and linkers improve Organisation.

quiz.question: For an FCE Part 2 letter or email you should:

options.d: Use an appropriate greeting and sign-off and match the register

quiz.explanation: Letters and emails need a greeting, sign-off and the right register.

quiz.question: Before you write, you should:

options.a: Plan and check you address every bullet

quiz.explanation: Planning and covering every bullet point help Content and Organisation.

quiz.question: Writing far over the word limit can:

options.d: Mean the examiner may not mark the extra

quiz.explanation: Examiners may not mark content beyond the limit.

quiz.question: Why might a single long paragraph with no linkers lose marks?
quiz.explanation: Organisation marks structure, paragraphs and linking.

sections.title: Writing exams: FCE, CAE, CPE
sections.content:
The Cambridge Writing paper has two tasks. You must do both.

**FCE (B2):** Part 1 is an essay (140–190 words). Part 2 is a choice of tasks – letter/email, article, review, story – (140–190 words). 1 hour 20 minutes.

**CAE (C1):** Part 1 is a compulsory essay (220–260 words). Part 2 is a choice of tasks – letter, proposal, report, review – (220–260 words). 1 hour 30 minutes.

**CPE (C2):** Part 1 is a compulsory essay (240–280 words). Part 2 is a choice of tasks – article, letter, report, review – (280–320 words). 1 hour 30 minutes.

Staying within the word count and following the task instructions are essential.

sections.title: Grading criteria
sections.content:
Scripts are marked using four criteria. Each carries equal weight (e.g. 5 marks each at FCE).

**Content** – Have you done what the task asked? Have you covered all points and added relevant ideas?

**Communicative Achievement** – Is the register (formal/informal) right? Is the format correct (e.g. letter, article)? Would the reader be informed and engaged?

**Organisation** – Is the text well structured with clear paragraphs, linking words, and a logical order?

**Language** – Have you used a range of grammar and vocabulary? Are they accurate? Spelling and punctuation count.

sections.title: How to gain marks
sections.content:
**Content:** Read the task and the input material carefully. Address every bullet point. Add your own ideas where the task invites them.

**Communicative Achievement:** Match the register to the task (e.g. formal for a report, informal for a letter to a friend). Use the correct layout (greeting/sign-off for letters, clear heading for reports).

**Organisation:** Plan before you write. Use paragraphs (e.g. one per main point). Use linkers (however, in addition, as a result) to connect ideas.

**Language:** Use a variety of structures and vocabulary. Check grammar and spelling. Use punctuation correctly (full stops, commas, apostrophes).

sections.title: How to avoid losing marks
sections.content:
**Content:** Do not go off-topic. Do not ignore part of the task. Do not write too little (you will lose marks) or way over the limit (examiners may not mark the extra).

**Communicative Achievement:** Do not use the wrong register (e.g. very informal in a formal report). Do not use the wrong format (e.g. no greeting in a letter when one is expected).

**Organisation:** Avoid a single long paragraph or a list of unconnected sentences. Avoid overusing the same linker (e.g. "and" or "also" only).

**Language:** Avoid repeated grammar or spelling mistakes. Avoid very basic vocabulary if you are aiming for a higher grade. Small errors (e.g. wrong preposition, wrong phrase) can add up – so check fixed phrases (e.g. at the end vs in the end) and common confusions.

commonErrors.wrong: In the end I am going to write about...
commonErrors.right: At the end (of the day / of this section) I am going to write about...
commonErrors.explanation: **In the end** = finally; after everything (e.g. We argued for hours. In the end, we agreed). **At the end** = at the final part of something (e.g. at the end of the essay, at the end of the day). When you mean 'at the end of the essay' or 'when I finish this part', use **at the end**.
